feed.title: JSatos
feed.subtitle: A minimal, responsive, and powerful Jekyll theme for presenting professional writing.

entry.title: Faisceaux: Sheaf of Modules
entry.summary: In establishing what scheme is, we’ve had to use some of the language of sheaves applied to, specifically, the category of rings, where we have a sheaf of rings $\mathscr O _X$ on some topological space $X$. However, as in learning commutative algebra, we should also discuss what sheaves produce when talked about in the sense of modules—modules are essential in the study of rings; the category ...

entry.title: Algebraic Spaces & (Pre)Stacks
entry.summary: The theory of algebraic spaces and stacks arised from the study of moduli spaces in algebraic geometry. Loosely speaking, a moduli space is a space $\mathcal M$ where there is a $1:1$ correspondence between points of $\mathcal M$ and isomorphism classes of certain types of algebra-geometric objectcs. For example, the moduli space $\mathcal M$ could be a topological space, complex manifold, or a...

entry.title: Schemes I
entry.summary: Classical algebraic geometry’s central focus is the study of algebraic varieties, where spaces are locally described as being loci of sets of polynomial equations over an algebraically closed field—much work is typically done over $\cc$. From the previous post, we can define an affine algebraic $k$-variety (or simply affine variety) as an irreducible Zariski-closed subset of $k^n$ for an algebr...

entry.title: Faisceaux: Sheaf
entry.summary: Sheaves and sheaf were in invented by Jean Lerray while he was a war prisoner in Austra— following the WWII trend of doing advanced mathematics in prisons (Weil and the Riemann Hypothesis over finite fields). Essentially, the point of a sheaf is to keep track of local algebraic data for some topological space. For example, an algebraic variety is a space that looks locally like the zero set of ...

entry.title: Coffee Chapter on Elliptic Curves
entry.summary: If someone has had an interest in pure mathematics for some time, then it’s almost inevitable to have heard about elliptic curves at some point. My first time hearing about elliptic curves, which we will define concretely in a bit, was when I was in high school when, I think, I watched a video relating to Fermat’s Last Theorem (FLT) and had heard that they were central to the proof FLT. For the...
